original_abstract: The book explores how weak presidents directly or indirectly elected can still play a crucial role in the political arena. Weak presidents have been distinguished from strong ones by relying on the evaluation of the powers they display according to the constitution. Six Western European countries are analysed: Germany and Italy which present a consistent constitutional design (as weak presidents are elected indirectly), and Austria, Ireland, Finland and Portugal that present inconsistent constitutional design (as weak presidents are elected directly). In contrast to much of the existing comparative works on presidential powers and activism, the book emphasises the role and the use of informal powers beyond that of formal ones. In particular, a definition and a typology of informal powers are provided as well as an empirical investigation on informal presidential activism. The positive outcome of presidential informal interventions are studied by relying on an interactionist approach which combines presidency-centred as well as president centred-explanations. The book argues that when dealing with informal presidential activism the opportunity structure matters but presidential public support matters even more.
humanized_abstract: This book provides an innovative perspective on the study of the American presidency by investigating the political fate of weak presidents who were elected neither by the popular vote nor in a direct contest with another presidential candidate. These presidents have generally been characterized as weak because they rely on constitutional evaluation of the formal powers assigned to the presidency. Along these lines, the study analyses six Western European countries: Germany and Italy characterized by a consistent constitutional structure (due to the indirect election of the weak president) and Austria, Ireland, Finland and Portugal that are characterized by an inconsistent constitutional structure (due to the direct election of the weak president). Unlike much of the comparative literature on presidential powers and activism, this book focuses on informal presidential powers and their uses, as well as the definition and typology of such powers and an investigation into the empirical occurrence of presidential informal activism. It also analyses the positive effects of presidential informal interventions based on an interactionist perspective that integrates presidency-centred and president-centric explanations. The book concludes that although the opportunity structure is an important factor when presidential informal activism is evaluated, presidential public support is even more relevant.

Along these lines, the study analyses six Western European countries: Germany and Italy characterized by a consistent constitutional structure (due to the indirect election of the weak president) and Austria, Ireland, Finland and Portugal that are characterized by an inconsistent constitutional structure (due to the direct election of the weak president). Unlike much of the comparative literature on presidential powers and activism, this book focuses on informal presidential powers and their uses, as well as the definition and typology of such powers and an investigation into the empirical occurrence of presidential informal activism. It also analyses the positive effects of presidential informal interventions based on an interactionist perspective that integrates presidency-centred and president-centric explanations. The book concludes that although the opportunity structure is an important factor when presidential informal activism is evaluated, presidential public support is even more relevant.
